stem: Standard precautions are based on the principle that:

options.text: Only patients with known infections pose a risk
All blood and body fluids are potentially infectious
Infection control is only necessary during surgical procedures

explanation: Standard precautions are based on the assumption that all blood, body fluids, secretions, excretions, non-intact skin, and mucous membranes may contain transmissible infectious agents.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 2

stem: The single most effective method of preventing the spread of infection is:
explanation: Hand hygiene (handwashing or use of alcohol-based hand rub) is consistently recognized as the single most important practice for preventing the transmission of infectious agents in healthcare settings.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 3

stem: Personal protective equipment (PPE) for dental procedures includes all of the following EXCEPT:
explanation: Standard PPE for routine dental procedures includes gloves, mask, protective eyewear, and clinical attire. Sterile surgical gowns are only required for surgical procedures, not routine dental care.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 3

options.text: Between each patient and when torn or punctured
At the end of the morning and afternoon sessions

explanation: Gloves must be changed between patients, when torn or punctured during a procedure, and before touching clean surfaces or items. They should never be washed and reused.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 3

stem: Which type of hand hygiene is recommended before donning gloves for patient care?

options.text: Routine handwash with antimicrobial soap or alcohol-based hand rub
Hand hygiene is not required if gloves will be worn

explanation: A routine handwash with antimicrobial soap and water, or use of an alcohol-based hand rub, is recommended before donning gloves. Hand hygiene is always required before gloving.
source: Infection Control and Management of Hazardous Materials, 7th Ed., Ch. 3
